questions.subject: Physics
questions.subsection: Differences Between Electric Field and Magnetic Field
questions.structural_anchor: Differences Between Electric Field and Magnetic Field
questions.question: A magnetic field is measured in which SI unit?
questions.hint: Recall the SI unit specifically for magnetic field strength.
questions.explanation: The Tesla (T) is the SI unit of magnetic field strength, defined as one newton per ampere-meter. Option A (N/C) is the unit of electric field, not magnetic field.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Differences Between Electric Field and Magnetic Field
source_trace.structural_element: Differences Between Electric Field and Magnetic Field
source_trace.content_anchor: SI unit of magnetic field is Tesla

explanations_tiered.definition: The Tesla (T) is the SI derived unit of magnetic flux density or magnetic field strength.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Magnetic field strength quantifies the magnetic force per unit current per unit length. Tesla relates to Newton per ampere-meter.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of Tesla as the 'how strong' measure of a magnet, like degrees measure temperature.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: One Tesla equals one Newton of force per ampere of current per meter of wire, represented as \(1\, \mathrm{T} = 1\, \mathrm{N/(A\cdot m)}\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The Tesla is defined as \(\mathrm{T} = \frac{\mathrm{kg}}{\mathrm{s}^2 \cdot \mathrm{A}}\). In contrast, the electric field unit N/C arises from \(\vec{E} = \vec{F}/q\).
reasoning_steps: Recall the formula for magnetic force on a wire: \(F = BIL\).
Solve for B: \(B = F/(IL)\).
Recognize that \(\mathrm{N/(A\cdot m)}\) is defined as the Tesla.
misconceptions: Weber is the unit of magnetic flux, not magnetic field strength. Flux is field strength times area.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Tesla (T) = N/(A·m) = magnetic field strength SI unit.

glossary_entry.definition: The SI unit of magnetic field strength equal to one newton per ampere-meter.

flashcard.front: What is the SI unit of magnetic field strength?
flashcard.back: The Tesla (T). One Tesla equals one newton per ampere-meter: \(1\, \mathrm{T} = 1\, \mathrm{N/(A\cdot m)}\).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What unit measures magnetic field strength?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The SI unit of magnetic flux density, equal to one weber per square meter or one newton per ampere-meter.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Students often confuse Tesla (field) with Weber (flux). Emphasize that flux is field times area.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Describe the difference between electric field and magnetic field units
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Tesla is the SI unit of magnetic field (N/(A·m)). N/C is electric field.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subsection: Differences Between Electric Field and Magnetic Field
questions.structural_anchor: Differences Between Electric Field and Magnetic Field
questions.question: How do magnetic field lines differ from electric field lines in terms of their starting and ending points?

options.A: Magnetic field lines start on north poles and end on south poles
options.B: Magnetic field lines form continuous closed loops without beginning or end
options.C: Magnetic field lines start on positive charges and end on negative charges
options.D: Magnetic field lines start on south poles and end on north poles

questions.hint: Consider whether magnetic monopoles exist compared to electric charges.
questions.explanation: Magnetic field lines form closed loops because isolated magnetic poles (monopoles) do not exist. Option A describes a common misconception, but field lines continue inside the magnet from south to north.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Differences Between Electric Field and Magnetic Field
source_trace.structural_element: Differences Between Electric Field and Magnetic Field
source_trace.content_anchor: magnetic field line do not have starting and ending point

explanations_tiered.definition: Magnetic field lines are continuous closed loops that emerge from north poles and enter south poles outside the magnet, then continue inside from south to north.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Unlike electric field lines that begin and end on charges, magnetic field lines have no start or end due to the absence of magnetic monopoles.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine drawing a circle around a magnet that never starts or stops—it just loops around.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Outside a magnet, lines go N → S; inside, lines go S → N, forming closed loops. This differs from electric lines which start at + and end at -.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Gauss's law for magnetism states \(\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{A} = 0\), mathematically proving no isolated magnetic monopoles exist, hence field lines must close.
reasoning_steps: Recall that isolated magnetic poles do not exist.
If a line started at a point, that point would be a monopole.
Therefore lines cannot start or end; they must form closed loops.
misconceptions: Some believe field lines start at north and end at south externally, forgetting the internal path that closes the loop.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Magnetic field lines: closed loops, no start/end. Electric field lines: start/end on charges.

glossary_entry.definition: Magnetic field lines that form continuous loops without beginning or end, indicating the absence of magnetic monopoles.

flashcard.front: Why do magnetic field lines form closed loops?
flashcard.back: Because isolated magnetic poles (monopoles) do not exist, so lines cannot begin or end on a pole.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Do magnetic field lines have starting points?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: No. They form continuous closed loops with no beginning or end because magnetic monopoles do not exist.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Magnetic field lines that continue through the magnet from south to north, forming uninterrupted curves.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: B-field lines: closed loops (no monopoles). E-field lines: start/stop on charges.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use Gauss's law for magnetism to reinforce why lines cannot start or end.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Compare magnetic and electric field lines
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Electric field lines start on positive charges and end on negative charges; magnetic field lines form closed loops due to no isolated poles.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A small compass placed at a point in a magnetic field will always point in which direction relative to the field line at that point?

options.B: Opposite to the field line direction
options.C: Along the tangent to the field line
options.D: Radially outward from the field line

questions.hint: Think about how a compass needle aligns with the local magnetic field direction.
questions.explanation: The direction of a magnetic field at any point is tangent to the field line at that point, and a compass aligns with this tangent. Option A describes electric field behavior relative to equipotentials.

source_trace.content_anchor: direction of the magnetic field is tangent to the field line at any point in space

explanations_tiered.definition: The tangent to a magnetic field line at any point gives the direction of the magnetic field vector at that point.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Field lines are visual tools where the line's tangent indicates field direction and density indicates strength.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you draw a line through a field, a compass sits like a car on a road—pointing where the road goes at that spot.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The magnetic field vector \(\vec{B}\) is always tangent to the field line. A compass needle aligns with this tangent because the north pole experiences force along \(\vec{B}\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: From the Lorentz force law \(\vec{F} = q\vec{v} \times \vec{B}\), the direction of \(\vec{B}\) is defined by the force on a moving charge; field lines map this direction.
reasoning_steps: Field lines are constructed so that the tangent at any point equals the field direction.
A compass needle aligns its north pole with the net field direction.
Therefore the compass points along the tangent.
misconceptions: Some think a compass points perpendicular to field lines or toward the nearest pole, but it aligns with the tangent.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Compass points along tangent to magnetic field line at that point.

glossary_entry.definition: The direction of the magnetic field vector at a point, represented by the tangent to the field line at that point.

flashcard.front: How does a compass align with a magnetic field line?
flashcard.back: It points along the tangent to the field line at that point, indicating the direction of the magnetic field vector.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What tells you the direction of a magnetic field at a point on a field line?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: The tangent to the field line at that point gives the field direction.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A line that touches a curve at a single point without crossing it; used to indicate magnetic field direction.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: B-field direction = tangent to field line.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use iron filings or compass arrays to show tangency experimentally.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Describe properties of magnetic field lines
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. A compass aligns with the local magnetic field direction, which is tangent to the field line.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If two magnetic field lines crossed at a point in space, what would this imply about the magnetic field at that point?

options.B: The field has two different directions simultaneously

questions.hint: Consider whether a vector can point in two directions at once.
questions.explanation: Magnetic field lines never cross because the field at any point has a unique direction. Crossing would imply two different \(\vec{B}\) directions at the same point, which is impossible.

source_trace.content_anchor: Magnetic field lines can never cross each other, meaning that the field is unique at any point in space

explanations_tiered.definition: Magnetic field lines are constructed so that at any point, the tangent gives the field direction; crossing would give two tangents.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The uniqueness of the magnetic field vector at each point forces field lines to never intersect.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If two streets crossed, a car could go two ways at once—impossible. Same for field lines and a compass.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: At any point in space, the magnetic field \(\vec{B}\) has a single magnitude and direction. Crossing field lines would imply two different directions at that point.
explanations_tiered.advanced: From the divergence-free condition \(\nabla \cdot \vec{B} = 0\), field lines cannot terminate or bifurcate in a way that creates crossings without violating uniqueness.
reasoning_steps: Assume two field lines cross at point P.
At P, line 1 defines direction \(\hat{d}_1\) and line 2 defines direction \(\hat{d}_2\).
The magnetic field would have to point in both directions simultaneously.
Contradiction; therefore lines cannot cross.
misconceptions: Some think crossing indicates a null point (zero field), but null points have no defined direction—crossing would still require two directions.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Magnetic field lines never cross: unique \(\vec{B}\) at every point.

glossary_entry.definition: Property of magnetic field lines that they cannot intersect because the magnetic field has a unique direction at any point.

flashcard.front: Why can't magnetic field lines cross?
flashcard.back: Crossing would imply two different magnetic field directions at the same point, which is impossible.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What would crossing magnetic field lines imply?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Crossing would mean two different field directions at the same point, which is impossible.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A property of vector fields where at any point there is exactly one magnitude and one direction.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: No crossing = unique \(\vec{B}\) at each point.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use a vector field plot to show that lines are just visual aids; the underlying field is single-valued.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Describe properties of magnetic field lines
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Crossing would give two different directions for \(\vec{B}\) at the same point, violating uniqueness.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: According to Ampere's law, the magnetic field created by an electric current is proportional to which quantity?

options.C: The size of the electric current

questions.hint: Recall the direct proportionality stated in Ampere's law.
questions.explanation: Ampere's law states that the magnetic field is proportional to the electric current, with constant of proportionality equal to the permeability of free space. Option A and D affect field geometry but not the direct proportionality.

source_trace.content_anchor: The magnetic field created by an electric current is proportional to the size of that electric current

explanations_tiered.definition: Ampere's law states that the line integral of magnetic field around a closed loop equals \(\mu_0\) times the current enclosed.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For a long straight wire, \(B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}\), showing direct proportionality to current \(I\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you double the current in a wire, the magnetic field around it also doubles.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Ampere's law gives \(\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I_{\text{enc}}\), so \(B \propto I\) for fixed geometry.
explanations_tiered.advanced: From the differential form \(\nabla \times \vec{B} = \mu_0 \vec{J}\), the curl of \(\vec{B}\) is directly proportional to current density, confirming \(B \propto I\).
reasoning_steps: Identify the closed loop around the current.
Apply \(\oint B \, dl = \mu_0 I\).
For symmetric cases, \(B \times \text{(path length)} = \mu_0 I\).
Thus \(B \propto I\).
misconceptions: Some confuse proportionality with voltage or resistance; Ampere's law directly links B and I without V or R.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Ampere's law: \(\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I\) → B ∝ I.

glossary_entry.definition: A law stating that the magnetic field around a current is proportional to the current with constant \(\mu_0\).

flashcard.back: Magnetic field B is proportional to current I: \(B \propto I\), with constant \(\mu_0\).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What does Ampere's law say B is proportional to?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: The electric current I. \(\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I\).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The magnetic field around a current-carrying conductor is proportional to the current.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Ampere: \(\oint B dl = \mu_0 I\) → B ∝ I.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that \(\mu_0\) is a constant (permeability of free space).
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Ampere's law directly states B ∝ I with constant \(\mu_0\).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subsection: Magnetic Field Created by a Long Straight Current-Carrying Wire
questions.structural_anchor: Magnetic Field Created by a Long Straight Current-Carrying Wire
questions.question: A long straight wire carries a current of \(5.0\, \mathrm{A}\). Calculate the magnetic field at a distance of \(0.10\, \mathrm{m}\) from the wire. (Use \(\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\, \mathrm{T\cdot m/A}\))
questions.hint: Use \(B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}\) and substitute values.
questions.explanation: Using \(B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r} = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7})(5.0)}{2\pi (0.10)} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{-6}}{0.10} = 1.0 \times 10^{-5}\, \mathrm{T}\). Option C uses \(r\) incorrectly in denominator.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Magnetic Field Created by a Long Straight Current-Carrying Wire
source_trace.structural_element: Magnetic Field Created by a Long Straight Current-Carrying Wire

explanations_tiered.definition: Magnetic field around a long straight wire is given by \(B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}\), where \(\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\, \mathrm{T\cdot m/A}\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The field decreases with distance from the wire and increases with current. At \(r = 0.10\, \mathrm{m}\) and \(I = 5.0\, \mathrm{A}\), the result is \(1.0 \times 10^{-5}\, \mathrm{T}\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Like sound getting quieter as you walk away, the magnetic field weakens with distance from the wire.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The derivation from Ampere's law: \(\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = B(2\pi r) = \mu_0 I \Rightarrow B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}\).
reasoning_steps: Identify knowns: I = 5.0 A, r = 0.10 m, μ₀ = 4π×10⁻⁷.
Select formula for long straight wire.
Substitute and simplify constants (4π/2π = 2).

formula_analysis.name: Magnetic field of long straight wire
formula_analysis.derivation: From Ampere's law \(\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I\) with circular path radius r.

misconceptions: Some forget to convert distance to meters; using 0.10 m (not 10 cm) is correct.
Using \(B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2r}\) (missing π) gives wrong magnitude.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Long straight wire: \(B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}\), B ∝ 1/r.

glossary_entry.definition: The magnetic field around an infinitely long straight wire is circular and given by \(B = \mu_0 I / (2\pi r)\).

flashcard.front: Formula for B at distance r from a long straight wire carrying current I?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Calculate B from a wire with I=5.0A at r=0.10m.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Magnetic field magnitude decreases as 1/r from a long straight current-carrying wire.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Common mistake: using r in cm without converting to meters.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate magnetic field from a long straight current-carrying wire

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subsection: Magnetic Field Created by a Long Straight Current-Carrying Wire
questions.structural_anchor: Magnetic Field Created by a Long Straight Current-Carrying Wire
questions.question: What happens to the direction of the magnetic field around a straight current-carrying wire when the current direction is reversed?

options.A: The field direction remains the same
options.D: The field becomes perpendicular to its original direction

questions.hint: Apply the right-hand rule to both current directions.
questions.explanation: Reversing the current reverses the magnetic field direction because \(\vec{B}\) depends linearly on \(\vec{I}\) via the Biot-Savart law. Option A would violate the right-hand rule.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Magnetic Field Created by a Long Straight Current-Carrying Wire
source_trace.structural_element: Magnetic Field Created by a Long Straight Current-Carrying Wire
source_trace.content_anchor: When the direction of the current is reversed, the direction of the magnetic field is reversed too

explanations_tiered.definition: The direction of the magnetic field around a wire is given by the right-hand rule and reverses when current reverses.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: From Biot-Savart, \(d\vec{B} \propto I\, d\vec{l} \times \hat{r}\); reversing I reverses \(d\vec{B}\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you imagine the field as a circle around the wire, flipping the current direction makes the circle spin the other way.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Right-hand rule: thumb in current direction, fingers curl in field direction. Reverse thumb → fingers curl opposite.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The cross product \(d\vec{l} \times \hat{r}\) changes sign when \(d\vec{l}\) reverses (since current direction changes), so \(\vec{B}\) reverses.
reasoning_steps: Point thumb of right hand in original current direction.
Observe curl of fingers = field direction.
Reverse thumb direction (current reversed).
Observe fingers curl opposite direction.
misconceptions: Some think field disappears when current reverses; field magnitude stays same, direction flips.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Reverse current → reverse magnetic field direction.

glossary_entry.definition: Reversing the direction of electric current in a wire reverses the direction of the magnetic field around it.

flashcard.front: What happens to B-field direction when current reverses in a straight wire?
flashcard.back: The magnetic field direction reverses as well, following the right-hand rule.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Does reversing current in a wire reverse the magnetic field?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Yes. The magnetic field direction reverses because B ∝ I.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Changing the direction of current flow, which reverses the associated magnetic field direction.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Demonstrate with a compass and a straight wire; reversing battery terminals flips compass deflection.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain the relationship between current and magnetic field direction
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Right-hand rule: thumb points with current, fingers curl with B. Reverse current → reverse B.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subsection: Magnetic Field Created by a Long Straight Current-Carrying Wire
questions.structural_anchor: Magnetic Field Created by a Long Straight Current-Carrying Wire
questions.question: A student claims that doubling both the current in a long straight wire and the distance from the wire will keep the magnetic field the same. Is this claim correct?

options.A: Yes, because \(B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}\) shows B depends on the ratio I/r
options.B: No, because doubling I doubles B but doubling r halves B, giving net change
options.C: Yes, because I and r are independent
options.D: No, because B depends on I²/r

questions.hint: Write \(B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}\) and see what happens when I→2I and r→2r.
questions.explanation: Doubling I doubles B; doubling r halves B. Combined effect: \(B' = \frac{\mu_0 (2I)}{2\pi (2r)} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r} = B\). The claim is actually correct; the error is in option B's reasoning.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Magnetic Field Created by a Long Straight Current-Carrying Wire
source_trace.structural_element: Magnetic Field Created by a Long Straight Current-Carrying Wire

explanations_tiered.definition: The magnetic field around a long straight wire is proportional to current and inversely proportional to distance.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Because B ∝ I/r, doubling both I and r leaves I/r unchanged, so B remains the same.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you double the current and also double how far away you measure, the field strength stays exactly the same.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: From \(B = \mu_0 I/(2\pi r)\), substituting \(I' = 2I\) and \(r' = 2r\) gives \(B' = \mu_0 (2I)/(2\pi (2r)) = \mu_0 I/(2\pi r) = B\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: This invariance holds because B scales as I/r; scaling both numerator and denominator equally leaves the quotient unchanged, regardless of the constant factor \(\mu_0/(2\pi)\).
reasoning_steps: Write B formula.
Replace I with 2I.
Replace r with 2r.
Simplify: (2)/(2) = 1, so B unchanged.
misconceptions: The misconception is that doubling both changes B; actually B stays the same because I/r constant. Option B incorrectly claims net change.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: B ∝ I/r. Double both I and r → B unchanged.

glossary_entry.definition: Property of B = μ₀I/(2πr) where scaling I and r equally leaves B unchanged.

flashcard.front: Does doubling both I and r in B = μ₀I/(2πr) keep B the same?
flashcard.back: Yes. B ∝ I/r, so doubling both leaves I/r unchanged, thus B unchanged.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: If I and r both double, what happens to B for a long straight wire?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: B stays the same because B ∝ I/r and the ratio I/r remains unchanged.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The quotient of current over distance that determines magnetic field magnitude for a straight wire.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Double I & r → B unchanged (B ∝ I/r).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use this as a conceptual check: students often think B changes; show algebra that it's invariant.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Analyse the relationship between current, distance, and magnetic field
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A (Yes). The claim is correct: B = μ₀I/(2πr) gives B' = μ₀(2I)/(2π·2r) = B. Option B incorrectly claims net change.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subsection: Magnetic Field Created by a Long Straight Current-Carrying Wire
questions.structural_anchor: Magnetic Field Created by a Long Straight Current-Carrying Wire
questions.question: A long straight wire carries a current toward the east. A compass is placed directly north of the wire. Which direction will the compass needle point?
questions.hint: Use the right-hand rule: thumb east (current), fingers curl. North of wire: field direction?
questions.explanation: Right-hand rule: thumb east → fingers curl upward (north) on south side? Wait: For a current east, field lines circle clockwise when viewed from above? Let's recalc: Thumb east, fingers curl: south of wire field is down? Actually standard: field direction at a point is tangent to circle. North of wire, field is west.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Magnetic Field Created by a Long Straight Current-Carrying Wire
source_trace.structural_element: Magnetic Field Created by a Long Straight Current-Carrying Wire

explanations_tiered.definition: The right-hand rule determines magnetic field direction around a current-carrying wire.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For current east, the magnetic field circles the wire. At a point directly north, the field vector points west.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Wrap your right hand around the wire with thumb pointing east. Your fingers show the field circles. North of wire, your fingers point west.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Using \(\vec{B} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r} \hat{\theta}\) with \(\hat{\theta}\) from cross product: \(\hat{\theta}\) direction is west at north point for eastward current.
explanations_tiered.advanced: From Biot-Savart: \(d\vec{B} \propto I d\vec{l} \times \hat{r}\). For current east (+x), point north (+y): \(\hat{r} = \hat{y}\), \(d\vec{l} = \hat{x}\), cross product \(\hat{x} \times \hat{y} = \hat{z}\) (up). But that's for a segment? Actually for infinite wire, field is circumferential: at north point, field is west (-x).
reasoning_steps: Point right thumb in direction of current (east).
Curl fingers naturally.
At a point north of the wire, fingers point toward west.
Thus compass needle points west.
misconceptions: Some think field points north-south along the wire; the field is actually circular around the wire.
Confusing the direction for points north vs south of the wire.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Current east → north of wire: B west.

glossary_entry.definition: Thumb points in current direction; curled fingers show magnetic field circulation direction.

flashcard.front: Current east; compass north of wire. Which way does it point?
flashcard.back: West. Right-hand rule: thumb east, fingers curl: north of wire → field west.

visual_system.visual_description: A straight wire with current arrow pointing east. A compass north of the wire shows needle pointing west.
visual_system.caption: Current east; north of wire, B field west.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Diagram: horizontal wire with eastward current arrow. North of wire, a compass needle points left (west). Circular dashed lines show field lines.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Current east, compass north of wire. Direction?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: West (right-hand rule: thumb east, fingers curl → north point field west).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Rule to find magnetic field direction around a current: thumb = current, fingers = field.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Current east, north point → B west.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Draw the wire and use a 3D right-hand model to show curling.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply the right-hand rule to determine magnetic field direction
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer D. Right-hand rule: thumb east, fingers curl; north of wire, field points west.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subsection: Magnetic Field Created by a Long Straight Current-Carrying Wire
questions.structural_anchor: Magnetic Field Created by a Long Straight Current-Carrying Wire
questions.question: A long straight wire produces a magnetic field of \(2.0 \times 10^{-5}\, \mathrm{T}\) at a distance of \(0.20\, \mathrm{m}\). What is the current in the wire? (\(\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\, \mathrm{T\cdot m/A}\))
questions.hint: Rearrange \(B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}\) to solve for I.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Magnetic Field Created by a Long Straight Current-Carrying Wire
source_trace.structural_element: Magnetic Field Created by a Long Straight Current-Carrying Wire

explanations_tiered.definition: The current in a long straight wire can be found from \(I = \frac{2\pi r B}{\mu_0}\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Rearranging the magnetic field formula isolates I. Substituting given values yields 20 A.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you know how strong the magnetic field is at a certain distance, you can calculate how much current must be flowing.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: From \(B = \mu_0 I/(2\pi r)\), multiply both sides by \(2\pi r\) and divide by \(\mu_0\): \(I = 2\pi r B / \mu_0\).
worked_solution: Step 2: Solve for I: \(I = \frac{2\pi r B}{\mu_0}\).
reasoning_steps: Write the formula for B around a straight wire.
Rearrange to solve for current I.
Substitute known values (r, B, μ₀).
Simplify the constants (π cancels).
Compute the numerical result.

formula_analysis.name: Magnetic field of long straight wire

misconceptions: Forgetting to convert distance to meters if given in cm.
Mistakenly using \(I = \frac{2\pi B}{\mu_0 r}\) (inverting r incorrectly).

glossary_entry.definition: The current in a long straight wire can be determined if the magnetic field at a known distance is measured.

flashcard.front: Given B=2.0×10⁻⁵T at r=0.20m from a straight wire, find I.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: B=2.0×10⁻⁵T at r=0.20m from straight wire. Find I.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Determining electric current from measured magnetic field and distance using Ampere's law.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Remind students to check unit cancellations; π cancels nicely.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate current from magnetic field measurements
